purpose: Self-relocating macOS Finder launcher. Designed to be moved to the Desktop, Dock, or anywhere else after first run ‚Äî on first launch it remembers the Synapse repo location in `$HOME/.synapse-path`, and on subsequent launches it reads that file to cd into the repo regardless of where the .command lives. Sources the user's zsh/bash profile and adds Homebrew paths to PATH so npm/node are available even when Finder spawns the script with a minimal environment, then runs `npm install` if needed and `npm run dev`.
gotchas: Stores its config in `$HOME/.synapse-path` ‚Äî moving the Synapse repo directory after first launch will cause the script to error out and ask the user to delete that file.

First-run requirement is subtle: this file MUST first be executed from inside the Synapse folder (where package.json sits) so it can record the path; running it from the Desktop on first launch produces an error.

Sources `~/.zshrc` AND `~/.bash_profile` AND prepends `/opt/homebrew/bin:/usr/local/bin` ‚Äî this triple fallback handles Apple Silicon (Homebrew at /opt/homebrew), Intel Macs (Homebrew at /usr/local), and pure shell-profile setups, but it WILL clobber the user's PATH order in unexpected ways.
Runs `npm run dev` (development mode), not `npm start` ‚Äî packaging expectations differ from `Start Synapse.command`.
Has `chmod +x` already (-rwxr-xr-x), but Gatekeeper still blocks unsigned .command files on first open ‚Äî comment tells user to right-click ‚Üí Open.
patterns: Self-relocating launcher pattern: cache repo path in a dotfile so the launcher script can be relocated freely.
Profile-sourcing pattern: explicitly load `~/.zshrc` and `~/.bash_profile` because Finder's GUI launch context lacks them.
PATH-prepend pattern: ensure Homebrew bin dirs are present even if the user's profile didn't add them.
conventions: Shebang `#!/bin/bash` (despite zsh being macOS default ‚Äî bash is more portable here).
.command extension is the macOS Finder convention for double-clickable shell scripts.
Error messages prompt for keypress (`read -n 1`) so the Terminal window stays open for the user to read.
